name: Solar & Wind Scale-up
summary:
Solar and wind scale-up represents the matured vanguard of the energy transition, having moved from subsidy-dependent niches to the cheapest sources of new electricity generation in most of the world. Solar PV module costs have collapsed over 90% in the past decade while wind has seen comparable though less dramatic declines, driven by manufacturing scale (predominantly in China), turbine upscaling, and learning-curve effects. Global installed capacity now exceeds 2,000 GW combined, yet penetration of actual electricity generation remains only ~13-14%, revealing the gap between capacity additions and energy delivered — a function of intermittency, curtailment, and grid integration constraints.

The narrative is shifting from a cost-reduction story to a systems-integration story. The binding constraints are no longer panel or turbine prices but grid interconnection queues (over 1,500 GW awaiting connection in the US alone), permitting timelines, transmission build-out, and the need for flexible resources — storage, demand response, long-duration storage — to absorb rising renewable penetration without destabilizing markets. Negative pricing events in California, Germany, and parts of China signal that the next phase of value creation lies not in generation hardware but in orchestration: storage co-location, grid-forming inverters, virtual power plants, and market design reform. Investors who continue to underwrite pure solar/wind capacity plays without accounting for cannibalization effects and curtailment risk are mispricing the transition.
key_drivers: LCOE collapse: solar at $25-40/MWh and onshore wind at $30-50/MWh in favorable markets, making renewables the default least-cost option for new generation globally
Policy tailwinds: US Inflation Reduction Act ITC/PTC stacking, EU Green Deal and REPowerEU targets, China's 1,200 GW wind+solar by 2030 commitment creating sustained demand pull
Corporate procurement: RE100 commitments covering 400+ companies requiring ~500 TWh of additional clean power, driving PPA market depth and corporate direct investment
Manufacturing overcapacity: Chinese solar cell/module capacity at ~800-1,000 GW against ~600 GW global demand, creating deflationary pressure that benefits developers but crushes manufacturer margins
Grid interconnection bottleneck: 1,500+ GW in US queues alone with 5-7 year wait times, representing both a deployment drag and an investment opportunity in transmission and grid-edge solutions

Cannibalization and negative pricing: rising renewable penetration eroding capture prices (solar capture rates in California down to 50-60% of average market prices), forcing integration with storage and flexible demand
Electrification demand pull: EV adoption, heat pumps, green hydrogen, and data center/AI load growth creating structural new electricity demand that renewables are positioned to serve

Permitting and land-use friction: local opposition, agricultural land conflicts, and zoning constraints slowing deployment in mature markets, creating premium value for projects with secured grid and land rights

adoption_curve.stage: Early Majority (electricity generation share); Late Early Adopter (capacity additions share)
adoption_curve.growth_rate: 18-22% CAGR in installed capacity (2023-2026E); generation growth ~20% CAGR as fleet utilization and curtailment dynamics mature
inflection_points: Storage cost-parity threshold ($100-150/kWh for 4-hour lithium-ion systems) enabling solar+storage to compete as dispatchable capacity, already crossed in several markets

Grid flexibility inflection at ~30-40% variable renewable penetration where conventional market structures break down, forcing market design reform (real-time pricing, capacity markets, ancillary services monetization)
Long-duration storage commercialization (~2030) enabling penetration beyond ~50% without firming resources, unlocking the next S-curve phase
Permitting and transmission reform legislation (US, EU) that could compress interconnection timelines from 5-7 years to 2-3 years, releasing trapped pipeline value
adoption_curve.penetration_pct_note: Share of global electricity generation from wind + solar combined; capacity share is higher (~25%) reflecting lower capacity factors
